research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: Thanks for making time, Priya. You maintain the pipelines that feed Pulse, right? What does adoption look like from where you sit?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Yeah, I own most of the ingestion jobs. Honestly, from the inside it's rough. I want teams to use Pulse, but I also know exactly where the bodies are buried, so I get why people drift back to their own spreadsheets.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: Where are the bodies buried? Start wherever feels biggest.

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Freshness, easily. The sync into Pulse only runs every six hours, so the data is stale by design. When the growth team opens a dashboard at 9am, they're looking at numbers from 3am. They notice, and they stop trusting it for anything time-sensitive.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: Has that staleness actually changed how a team uses it?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Constantly. The sales ops folks ran their standup off Pulse for a week, then went back to a manual export because they couldn't trust intraday numbers. If the data is stale by design, you can't use it for the live decisions people actually care about.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: You mentioned trust. Is there anything beyond freshness eroding it?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Yeah, and this one's worse because it's silent. There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed. Someone in finance cross-checked revenue against the source system and it didn't match. Once that happens once, they question every number.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: How does the access side factor in, if at all?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: It's a huge drag. I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need. A new analyst joins, opens Pulse, and half the datasets are greyed out. By the time their permissions clear, they've already given up and asked me to just pull the data for them. Oh, and the metric naming is inconsistent between teams, 'active_users' means three different things, which drives me a little crazy too.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: If you could fix one of these tomorrow, which moves adoption most?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Fix the join bug first. Freshness and access are friction, but the double-count is the thing that quietly kills trust. If the numbers are off and people noticed, no amount of fresh data or smooth permissions will get them back. Earn the trust, then fix the rest.

summary: Priya, a data engineer who maintains Pulse's pipelines, attributes low adoption to a six-hour-stale sync, a known join bug that double-counts and erodes trust, and constant access-permission friction, naming the trust-killing data bug as the top fix.
